subquestions.question_text: State the alternative hypothesis for the critic's test that burgers weigh less than advertised.
Find the p-value for the one-sample t-test.
At the 10% significance level, what is the conclusion?

options.label: A
options.text: Do not reject \(H_0\). There is insufficient evidence that the mean burger weight is less than \(200\text{ g}\).

options.label: B
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). There is evidence that the mean weight is less than \(200\text{ g}\).

options.label: C
options.text: Accept \(H_0\) and prove the mean is exactly \(200\text{ g}\).

subquestions.question_text: State the hypotheses for testing whether Game B is rated higher than Game A.
Find the p-value for the two-sample t-test.
At the 5% level, what conclusion should be made?

options.label: A
options.text: Do not reject \(H_0\). There is no evidence Game B is better.

options.label: B
options.text: Accept \(H_0\). The games are exactly equal.

options.label: D
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). There is evidence that Game B is rated higher on average.

subquestions.question_text: State the hypotheses for testing whether the two fertilizers have different effects.
Find the p-value for this two-tailed two-sample t-test.
At \(\alpha=10\%\), what is the conclusion?

options.label: A
options.text: Do not reject \(H_0\). There is no evidence of a difference.

options.label: B
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). There is evidence that the fertilizer type makes a difference.

options.label: D
options.text: Accept \(H_0\). The fertilizers are proven equivalent.

subquestions.question_text: For 600 rolls of a fair six-sided die, find the expected frequency for each face.
State the hypotheses for the goodness-of-fit test for fairness of the die.

options.label: A
options.text: \(H_0:\) the die is fair; \(H_1:\) the die is not fair.

options.label: B
options.text: \(H_0:\) the die is not fair; \(H_1:\) the die is fair.

options.label: C
options.text: \(H_0:\) all observed frequencies are equal; \(H_1:\) all expected frequencies are zero.

options.label: D
options.text: \(H_0:\) the mean is 3.5; \(H_1:\) the mean is 6.

subquestions.question_text: Find the degrees of freedom for the die goodness-of-fit test.
Find the p-value for the goodness-of-fit test.
At the 5% level, state the conclusion.

options.label: D
options.text: Do not reject \(H_0\). There is insufficient evidence that the die is unfair.

subquestions.question_text: State the hypotheses for testing whether the participant's estimated schedule is accurate.

options.label: A
options.text: \(H_0:\) the time distribution follows the participant's estimate; \(H_1:\) it does not.

options.label: B
options.text: \(H_0:\) all activities take equal time; \(H_1:\) they do not.

options.label: C
options.text: \(H_0:\) the watch is inaccurate; \(H_1:\) the watch is accurate.

options.label: D
options.text: \(H_0:\) the observed data are random; \(H_1:\) they are fixed.

subquestions.question_text: Find the expected times for Work, Chores, Hobbies, Socializing, and Sleeping.
Find the p-value for the goodness-of-fit test.
At the 10% level, state the conclusion.

options.label: A
options.text: Do not reject \(H_0\). The estimate is supported.

options.label: D
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). The distribution is different from the participant's estimate.

subquestions.question_text: Would the conclusion change at a 5% significance level?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes. At \(5\%\), we would not reject \(H_0\).

options.label: D
options.text: No. The p-value changes with the significance level.

subquestions.question_text: If attendance is equal across four concert dates, find the expected attendance for each date.
Find the degrees of freedom for the goodness-of-fit test.
Find the \(\chi^2\) statistic for this test.
Using the critical value \(7.81\), state the conclusion.

options.label: D
options.text: Accept \(H_0\), since the expected values are equal.

subquestions.question_text: State the hypotheses for testing whether vegetable preference is influenced by city.

options.label: A
options.text: \(H_0:\) preference is not independent of city; \(H_1:\) preference is independent of city.

options.label: B
options.text: \(H_0:\) all vegetables are equally preferred; \(H_1:\) all cities are equal.

options.label: C
options.text: \(H_0:\) preference is independent of city; \(H_1:\) preference is not independent of city.

subquestions.question_text: Find the degrees of freedom for the independence test.
Find the p-value for the test.
At the 5% level, state the conclusion.

options.label: A
options.text: Do not reject \(H_0\). There is insufficient evidence that vegetable preference depends on city.

subquestions.question_text: State the hypotheses for testing whether activity level is independent of age group.

options.label: A
options.text: \(H_0:\) activity level depends on age group; \(H_1:\) it is independent.

options.label: B
options.text: \(H_0:\) all groups are very active; \(H_1:\) no groups are active.

options.label: C
options.text: \(H_0:\) activity level is independent of age group; \(H_1:\) activity level is not independent of age group.

subquestions.question_text: Find the degrees of freedom for this test.
Find the \(\chi^2\) statistic for this test.
Using the critical value \(12.59\), state the conclusion.

options.label: D
options.text: Accept \(H_0\), since the variables are independent.

subquestions.question_text: Find the missing probability of rolling a 5.
Find the probability of rolling an even number.
Given that an even number is rolled, find the probability that it is a 6.
Show the expected frequency of rolling a 6 in 100 throws.
At the 5% level, state the conclusion of the \(\chi^2\) goodness-of-fit test, given \(p=0.491\).

options.label: A
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). The shop probabilities are incorrect.

options.label: C
options.text: Do not reject \(H_0\). There is insufficient evidence that the shop probabilities are incorrect.

options.label: D
options.text: Accept \(H_1\). The die is loaded differently.

subquestions.question_text: State the hypotheses for testing whether the new training programme increases mean jump height.
Find the p-value for the two-sample t-test.
At the 5% level, state the conclusion.

options.label: A
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). There is sufficient evidence that the new programme increases mean jump height.

options.label: B
options.text: Do not reject \(H_0\). There is insufficient evidence.

options.label: C
options.text: Accept \(H_0\). The programmes are the same.

options.label: D
options.text: Reject \(H_1\). The original programme is better.

subquestions.question_text: Find the number of households that own at least 4 bicycles.
Find the probability that a randomly selected household owns fewer than 3 bicycles.
Find the mode of the bicycle distribution.
Find the median number of bicycles.
Find the interquartile range.
State the null hypothesis for the holiday preference and age group test.

options.label: A
options.text: Holiday preference and age group are not independent.

options.label: B
options.text: Holiday preference and age group are independent.

options.label: D
options.text: All age groups have the same size.

subquestions.question_text: State the alternative hypothesis.

options.label: A
options.text: Holiday preference and age group are independent.

options.label: D
options.text: Holiday preference and age group are not independent.

subquestions.question_text: Find the degrees of freedom for the test of independence.
Find the expected frequency for adults aged 30–49 who prefer city holidays.
Find the p-value for the test.
State the conclusion of the test.

options.label: A
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). Holiday preference depends on age group.

options.label: D
options.text: Fail to reject \(H_0\). There is insufficient evidence that holiday preference depends on age group.

subquestions.question_text: Complete the expected frequency table if 150 emails are equally distributed across 5 weekdays.
State the hypotheses for testing whether emails are uniformly distributed across weekdays.

options.label: A
options.text: \(H_0:\) emails are not uniformly distributed; \(H_1:\) emails are uniformly distributed.

options.label: B
options.text: \(H_0:\) Monday has the most emails; \(H_1:\) Friday has the most emails.

options.label: C
options.text: \(H_0:\) emails are uniformly distributed; \(H_1:\) emails are not uniformly distributed.

subquestions.question_text: Find the degrees of freedom.
Find the \(\chi^2\) statistic and p-value.
Using the critical value \(9.488\), state the conclusion.

options.label: D
options.text: Accept \(H_1\), since emails are not uniform.

subquestions.question_text: Find \(P(V<990)\) for carton volumes modelled by \(N(1000,6^2)\).
Estimate the expected number of cartons with \(990\le V<1000\) in a sample of 600.
Estimate the expected number of cartons with \(1000\le V<1012\) in a sample of 600.
State the hypotheses for the goodness-of-fit test on carton volumes.

options.label: A
options.text: \(H_0:\) volumes do not follow \(N(1000,6^2)\); \(H_1:\) they do.

options.label: C
options.text: \(H_0:\) volumes follow \(N(1000,6^2)\); \(H_1:\) volumes do not follow \(N(1000,6^2)\).

options.label: D
options.text: \(H_0:\) all observed frequencies are equal; \(H_1:\) they are not.

subquestions.question_text: Find the p-value of the goodness-of-fit test.
State the conclusion at the 5% level.

options.label: C
options.text: Accept \(H_0\), since the model is exact.

subquestions.question_text: Find the missing value \(k\) in the age 10 row.
Find the degrees of freedom for the activity preference and age test.
Find the \(\chi^2\) statistic.
Using the critical value \(12.6\), state the conclusion.

options.label: A
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). Activity preference depends on age.

options.label: D
options.text: Fail to reject \(H_0\). There is insufficient evidence that activity preference depends on age.

subquestions.question_text: State the null hypothesis for comparing reaction times between breakfast groups.
State the alternative hypothesis.
Find the p-value for the two-sample t-test.
At the 10% level, does the teacher's conclusion agree with the study?

options.label: B
options.text: Yes. Accept \(H_0\); the means are equal.

options.label: D
options.text: Yes. Reject \(H_0\); there is evidence of a significant difference in mean reaction time.

subquestions.question_text: State the hypotheses for testing whether employees are equally likely to choose any of the six offices.

options.label: A
options.text: \(H_0:\) employees are equally likely to choose each office; \(H_1:\) they are not equally likely.

options.label: B
options.text: \(H_0:\) employees are not equally likely; \(H_1:\) they are equally likely.

options.label: C
options.text: \(H_0:\) all offices have different frequencies; \(H_1:\) all offices are the same.

subquestions.question_text: Determine the expected number of employees assigned to each office.
Find the degrees of freedom for this test.
Given \(p\approx0.117\), state the conclusion at the 5% level.

options.label: A
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). Employees are not equally likely to choose the offices.

options.label: B
options.text: Accept \(H_1\). The distribution is not uniform.

options.label: D
options.text: Fail to reject \(H_0\). There is insufficient evidence that employees are not equally likely to choose the six offices.

subquestions.question_text: Construct the probability distribution for the four spending categories.

options.label: A
options.text: Upgrades \(\frac{12}{25}\), special items \(\frac{6}{25}\), extra lives \(\frac{4}{25}\), saving coins \(\frac{3}{25}\).

options.label: B
options.text: Upgrades \(\frac{6}{25}\), special items \(\frac{12}{25}\), extra lives \(\frac{4}{25}\), saving coins \(\frac{3}{25}\).

options.label: C
options.text: Upgrades \(\frac{12}{25}\), special items \(\frac{4}{25}\), extra lives \(\frac{6}{25}\), saving coins \(\frac{3}{25}\).

subquestions.question_text: Find the probability of exactly 3 purchases on special items out of 12.
Find the probability of at least 5 purchases on upgrades out of 12.
Determine the expected frequencies for 400 purchases.
State the hypotheses for the goodness-of-fit test.

options.label: A
options.text: \(H_0:\) categories follow the model; \(H_1:\) categories do not follow the model.

options.label: B
options.text: \(H_0:\) categories do not follow the model; \(H_1:\) categories follow it.

options.label: C
options.text: \(H_0:\) all categories are equal; \(H_1:\) all categories are different.

options.label: D
options.text: \(H_0:\) purchases are independent; \(H_1:\) purchases are dependent.

subquestions.question_text: Find the degrees of freedom.
Find the p-value for the goodness-of-fit test.
State the conclusion at the 5% level.

options.label: A
options.text: Fail to reject \(H_0\). The model is suitable.

options.label: B
options.text: Accept \(H_0\). The observed values match exactly.

options.label: D
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). There is sufficient evidence that the company's model is not suitable.

subquestions.question_text: State the null hypothesis for comparing mean running times of the two programmes.
State the alternative hypothesis.
Find the t-statistic and p-value.
At the 5% level, state the conclusion.

options.label: A
options.text: Fail to reject \(H_0\). There is no significant difference.

options.label: C
options.text: Reject \(H_1\). The programmes are the same.

options.label: D
options.text: Reject \(H_0\). There is sufficient evidence that the mean running times are different.
